name: Tokyo
desc: In 1923, the Great Kanto Earthquake flattened Tokyo and killed over 100,000 people. In the chaos that followed, rumours spread that Korean residents had poisoned the water supply. It was entirely false — but vigilante mobs massacred an estimated 6,000 Korean civilians before the military restored order. The Japanese government suppressed the story for decades. A small memorial in a Tokyo park is the only public acknowledgment of one of the darkest episodes in the city's modern history.

name: Delhi
desc: In 1739, Persian emperor Nader Shah invaded Delhi and demanded the city's surrender. When a rumour spread that he had been killed, Delhi's residents attacked his troops. In retaliation, Nader Shah ordered a massacre — his soldiers killed between 20,000 and 30,000 civilians in a single day. He then looted the city and took the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond back to Persia. Delhi never fully recovered its former wealth, and the Mughal Empire began its terminal decline that same afternoon.

name: Shanghai
desc: In 1987, Shanghai authorities discovered that the city's beloved French Concession plane trees — 150,000 of them, planted in the 1900s — were dying from a root fungus. Rather than remove them, the city spent years injecting each tree individually with antifungal solution. The trees survived. Today those same century-old sycamores still arch over the old French quarter streets, their roots buckling the pavement, protected by city ordinance. Residents have been known to chain themselves to trees threatened by construction.

name: São Paulo
desc: In 2006, a criminal organisation called the PCC launched a coordinated attack across São Paulo from inside prison — ordering simultaneous strikes on police stations, banks, and buses across the city. Over three days, 40 police officers and 82 civilians were killed, and much of the city ground to a halt. The government eventually negotiated with the imprisoned gang leaders to end the violence. The episode revealed that Brazil's largest city was effectively held hostage by an organisation operating from behind bars.

name: Mexico City
desc: Mexico City's subway system regularly unearths Aztec artefacts during construction. But in 2020, workers excavating a new metro line discovered something extraordinary: over 650 human skulls arranged in a tower formation — a tzompantli, or skull rack — used by the Aztecs to display sacrificial victims. The tower was 4.7 metres in diameter and included the skulls of women and children, suggesting they were not only warriors. It sits directly beneath one of the world's busiest cities, which has been built entirely on top of a vanished civilisation.

name: Cairo
desc: In 1798, Napoleon invaded Egypt and brought 150 scientists, artists, and scholars with him — the first time a military expedition had included an academic team. They measured the pyramids, documented hieroglyphs, and catalogued Egyptian wildlife. They also found the Rosetta Stone. Napoleon's savants published their findings in the Description de l'Égypte, a 23-volume work that effectively invented the field of Egyptology. Napoleon lost Egypt two years later, but the academic legacy of his invasion changed how the world understood the ancient world.

name: Dhaka
desc: In 1971, during Bangladesh's war of independence from Pakistan, Pakistani forces carried out a systematic massacre of intellectuals in Dhaka — targeting professors, doctors, journalists, and engineers in a deliberate attempt to destroy the country's educated class. On the night of March 25 alone, hundreds were killed. The event is commemorated as Intellectual Martyrs Day every December. Bangladesh's founding generation effectively had to rebuild a nation's intellectual infrastructure from scratch, making the country's subsequent development all the more remarkable.

name: Mumbai
desc: In November 2008, ten gunmen arrived by sea from Pakistan and spread across Mumbai, attacking hotels, a train station, a Jewish centre, and a hospital. The siege lasted 60 hours and killed 166 people. One attacker was captured alive — Ajmal Kasab — and became the centrepiece of one of India's most expensive and elaborate criminal trials. He was hanged in 2012 in near-total secrecy, with the execution only announced hours later. The attacks fundamentally changed security arrangements across India's cities.

name: Beijing
desc: In 1900, a secret society called the Boxers besieged Beijing's foreign legation quarter for 55 days, supported by the Chinese imperial government. Around 900 foreign nationals and 3,000 Chinese Christians sheltered inside a compound barely a few blocks wide, improvising defences from furniture and sandbags. A multinational relief force of eight nations eventually broke through. The episode led to the Boxer Protocol — a humiliating treaty that required China to pay the equivalent of $10 billion in today's money to the foreign powers.

name: Osaka
desc: In 1970, Osaka hosted the World Expo — the first held in Asia — and built a centrepiece called the Tower of the Sun, a bizarre 70-metre sculpture by artist Taro Okamoto depicting three faces: one on top, one on the front, and one on the back representing the past. Okamoto refused to let the organisers demolish it after the expo and it still stands in Expo Commemorative Park. Hidden inside the tower, undiscovered until 2018, was an elaborate installation called the Tree of Life that had been sealed for 48 years.

name: New York
desc: In 1904, the General Slocum steamboat caught fire in New York's East River while carrying 1,300 German-American passengers on a church picnic. Over 1,000 people died — the deadliest disaster in New York City history until September 11, 2001. The life preservers were rotten, the fire hoses burst, and the captain beached the burning boat on an island rather than the closer Manhattan shore. The German neighbourhood of Kleindeutschland on the Lower East Side was so devastated by the losses that it effectively ceased to exist, its surviving residents scattered uptown.

name: Karachi
desc: In the 1980s, Karachi became the accidental recipient of an enormous wave of Afghan refugees and CIA-funded weapons flowing into Pakistan to arm the Mujahideen against the Soviets. Thousands of Kalashnikovs and rocket launchers leaked into the city's criminal underworld. By the 1990s, Karachi had become one of the world's most violent cities, with ethnic and political militias fighting gun battles in the streets. The violence peaked in 1995 when over 2,000 people were killed in a single year — a conflict almost entirely invisible to the outside world.

name: Chongqing
desc: During World War II, Chongqing was bombed by Japan more intensively than almost any city on earth — over 200 raids between 1938 and 1944. Residents built an extraordinary network of tunnels beneath the city for shelter. In one 1941 raid, a panicking crowd stampeded into a tunnel and the entrance was blocked — over 10,000 people suffocated or were crushed to death in what became known as the Chongqing Tunnel Disaster. The Chinese government suppressed the story; the full death toll only became publicly known decades later.

name: Istanbul
desc: In 1955, a fake news story spread across Istanbul claiming that Greeks in Thessaloniki had bombed the Turkish consulate and the birthplace of Mustafa Kemal Atatürk. Within hours, organised mobs were destroying Greek-owned shops, homes, and churches across the city. The pogrom lasted nine hours and drove most of Istanbul's remaining Greek population to emigrate. It later emerged that the rumour was deliberately fabricated by Turkish nationalists, and that the mobs had been transported by truck with prepared lists of Greek properties to target.

name: Buenos Aires
desc: In 1945, Buenos Aires hosted a reception for Adolf Eichmann, Josef Mengele, and hundreds of other Nazi war criminals who had escaped Europe through the so-called ratlines — escape routes facilitated by sympathetic Catholic clergy and Argentine diplomats. Argentina under Juan Perón was one of the primary destinations. Eichmann lived quietly in a Buenos Aires suburb under the name Ricardo Klement for fifteen years, until Israeli Mossad agents abducted him off a street in 1960, bundled him onto a plane, and flew him to Israel for trial.

name: Kolkata
desc: In 1943, a famine killed between two and three million people in Bengal — not because food was unavailable, but because British wartime policies had prioritised military supplies and exports. Winston Churchill refused to divert food shipments to India and reportedly blamed Indians for the famine by breeding like rabbits. Food was exported from Bengal even as people starved in Kolkata's streets. The famine remains one of the most contested events of the British Empire, with economists still debating how many deaths were directly caused by policy decisions.

name: Lagos
desc: In 2005, Lagos state government announced it would demolish Maroko, a waterfront slum of 300,000 people, to make way for luxury development. Residents had a week's notice. The demolitions proceeded regardless, leaving hundreds of thousands homeless overnight. The land eventually became the upmarket Victoria Island extension now occupied by some of the most expensive real estate in Africa. Maroko became a symbol of how Africa's fastest-growing megacities were developing — by erasing their poorest inhabitants rather than accommodating them.

name: Kinshasa
desc: In 1974, Kinshasa hosted the Rumble in the Jungle — the heavyweight boxing match between Muhammad Ali and George Foreman, organised by dictator Mobutu Sese Seko as a propaganda exercise to project Zaire's image internationally. Ali used his rope-a-dope strategy, letting Foreman exhaust himself for seven rounds before knocking him out in the eighth. Around 60,000 people packed the stadium at 4 AM to avoid the equatorial heat. The fight was watched by a global television audience and remains one of the greatest sporting events in history.

name: Manila
desc: In 1986, Manila witnessed one of history's most remarkable political events: the People Power Revolution. After Ferdinand Marcos declared victory in a clearly fraudulent election, millions of ordinary Filipinos took to EDSA highway and formed a human chain between the army and defecting military factions. Priests and nuns knelt in front of tanks. Marcos fled by US military helicopter after 21 years in power. The bloodless revolution became a template for peaceful regime change that influenced pro-democracy movements from Eastern Europe to Southeast Asia.

name: Tianjin
desc: On August 12, 2015, a warehouse storing 2,500 tonnes of ammonium nitrate and other chemicals exploded in Tianjin's port district, creating a blast so powerful it registered as a 2.9 magnitude earthquake and was visible from space. 173 people were killed and thousands of vehicles and shipping containers were vaporised. The warehouse had been operating illegally, too close to residential areas, with full knowledge of local officials who had accepted bribes to ignore safety violations. The disaster exposed systemic corruption in China's industrial regulation system.

name: Guangzhou
desc: In the 1850s, a local dispute in Guangzhou escalated into one of history's strangest wars. British officials seized a ship called the Arrow, claiming it was flying a British flag illegally. The Chinese governor refused to apologise. Britain and France used the incident as a pretext to attack Guangzhou and force China to open more ports to trade. The Second Opium War that followed ended with foreign troops marching into Beijing and burning the Summer Palace to the ground — all because of a quarrel about a flag on a small trading vessel.

name: Rio de Janeiro
desc: In 1904, Rio de Janeiro's government launched a mandatory smallpox vaccination campaign, sending health workers door-to-door to forcibly vaccinate residents. The poor and working class, deeply suspicious of a government that had recently abolished slavery, revolted. For ten days, rioters tore up tram rails, threw bottles at soldiers, and fought pitched battles with police. The Vaccine Revolt killed 30 people and toppled a military school cadet uprising that briefly seemed poised to overthrow the government — all triggered by a syringe.

name: Lahore
desc: In 1947, the Radcliffe Line — the border between India and Pakistan — was drawn by a British lawyer named Cyril Radcliffe who had never visited the subcontinent. He was given five weeks and a set of maps. His line cut through Lahore's outskirts, dividing villages, farmland, and irrigation canals. When the border was announced, one of the largest migrations in human history began: 14 million people crossed in both directions, and between 200,000 and 2 million were killed in the accompanying violence. Radcliffe burned his papers and never returned to the region.

name: Bangalore
desc: In 1791, Bangalore was the site of one of the most dramatic moments in British imperial history. Tipu Sultan, the Tiger of Mysore, laid siege to the city and fought the British East India Company in a series of wars using the world's first iron-cased military rockets — decades before such technology existed in Europe. Tipu died defending his capital Seringapatam in 1799. A lock of his hair, his sword, and his mechanical tiger automaton that devours a British soldier are still displayed in museums in London and Mysore.

name: Shenzhen
desc: In 1980, when Deng Xiaoping designated Shenzhen as China's first Special Economic Zone, he drew a literal line in the ground — a barbed wire fence separating the experimental capitalist enclave from the rest of communist China. For years, crossing from Hong Kong into Shenzhen was like crossing between worlds. People from the mainland would stand at the fence staring at Hong Kong's neon glow. Today that fence is gone, the fishing village has become a megacity of 12 million, and the experiment it represented has reshaped the global economy.

name: Moscow
desc: In 1812, Napoleon captured Moscow and waited five weeks for Tsar Alexander to surrender. The Tsar never came. Instead, Russian nobles set fire to their own city — burning 75 percent of Moscow's buildings to deny Napoleon the resources he needed to sustain his army through winter. Napoleon watched from the Kremlin as the city burned around him, then began the catastrophic retreat that destroyed his Grande Armée. Of the 600,000 soldiers who marched into Russia, fewer than 100,000 returned. Moscow was completely rebuilt within four years.

name: Chennai
desc: In December 2004, the Indian Ocean tsunami struck Chennai without warning on a Sunday morning when fishermen and beachgoers packed the Marina Beach — one of the longest urban beaches in the world. The wave killed over 200 people on the beach alone and devastated the fishing communities north and south of the city. But Chennai's response became a model for disaster management: the city rebuilt fishing villages faster than almost anywhere else in the tsunami-affected region, and the Marina Beach was reopened to the public within weeks.

name: Bogotá
desc: In the 1990s, Bogotá was one of the most dangerous cities on earth, with a murder rate of 80 per 100,000 people. Then a series of unconventional mayors transformed it. Antanas Mockus, a philosophy professor who once dropped his trousers at a student rally, replaced traffic police with mimes who shamed bad drivers with theatrical performances. He also distributed 350,000 thumbs-up and thumbs-down cards for citizens to signal approval or disapproval at each other in public. The murder rate fell by 70 percent over the following decade.

name: Paris
desc: In 1832, Paris was struck by a cholera epidemic that killed 20,000 people in six months — including the Prime Minister. The city was still under martial law from the 1830 revolution, and desperate residents began suspecting the government was poisoning the water supply to kill the poor. Paranoid mobs attacked people they believed were poisoners — including a man lynched for carrying a suspicious white powder that turned out to be sugar. The epidemic inspired Victor Hugo to incorporate the Paris sewers into Les Misérables as a symbol of the city's hidden suffering.

name: Jakarta
desc: Jakarta is sinking at one of the fastest rates of any city on earth — up to 25 centimetres per year in some districts, caused by millions of residents illegally pumping groundwater because the city's pipes don't reach them. North Jakarta, once a Dutch colonial harbour, is now largely below sea level and regularly floods even without rain. Residents have built their homes on stilts. The government announced it would move Indonesia's capital to Borneo partly because Jakarta may be largely uninhabitable within decades.

name: Lima
desc: In 1996, 14 members of the Túpac Amaru Revolutionary Movement stormed the Japanese ambassador's residence in Lima during a cocktail party and took 72 hostages, including government ministers and foreign diplomats. The siege lasted 126 days — one of the longest hostage crises in history. Peruvian commandos had spent months digging tunnels beneath the compound. When they finally stormed it, all 14 rebels were killed — several after surrendering, according to survivors. President Fujimori was later convicted of human rights violations related to the operation.

name: Bangkok
desc: In 2011, a monsoon season of unprecedented intensity sent floodwaters slowly engulfing Bangkok for months. Over a third of Thailand's provinces were inundated, and water crept toward the city for weeks as authorities desperately built sandbag barriers. The flooding killed 815 people and caused $45 billion in damage — including paralysing the world's hard drive manufacturing industry, since Bangkok's industrial estates produced 40 percent of global hard drives. Computer prices spiked worldwide. The crisis revealed how deeply a single city's flooding could disrupt the global technology supply chain.

name: Hyderabad
desc: In 1948, Hyderabad was an independent state the size of France, ruled by the Nizam — officially the world's richest man — who resisted joining the newly independent India. The Indian government launched Operation Polo: a military invasion euphemistically called a police action. In five days, the Nizam's army surrendered. What followed was a violent reckoning in which estimates of the dead range from 27,000 to 200,000. The episode was suppressed for decades; an Indian government report documenting the violence was classified until 2013.

name: Seoul
desc: In 1994, the Seongsu Bridge in Seoul collapsed during morning rush hour, killing 32 people. Investigators found that the city's rapid post-war growth had been accompanied by catastrophically shoddy construction — contractors had bribed inspectors and used substandard materials throughout. The following year, the Sampoong Department Store collapsed in a cascade of concrete, killing 502 people in one of history's deadliest building failures. The twin disasters forced a complete overhaul of South Korea's construction industry and began a national reckoning with the hidden costs of its economic miracle.

name: Nagoya
desc: In 1945, the US firebombed Nagoya's Mitsubishi aircraft factories — the same factories that had produced the Zero fighters used at Pearl Harbour. But American planners knew exactly what to target because Mitsubishi had sent engineering blueprints to the US before the war to license American machine tools, giving American intelligence a perfect map of the facilities. The bombing destroyed much of Nagoya. After the war, Nagoya rebuilt around Mitsubishi's automotive division, which became Toyota — arguably the most consequential industrial phoenix in history.

name: London
desc: In 1952, London was engulfed by the Great Smog — a dense, sulphurous fog caused by cold air trapping coal smoke from millions of chimneys and power stations. Visibility dropped to inches. Theatres closed because audiences couldn't see the stage. Cattle at Smithfield show were found dead in their pens. The smog lasted five days and killed an estimated 12,000 people — more than the Blitz killed in some months. The British government initially denied there was a crisis, then quietly revised the death toll upward. The Clean Air Act followed four years later.

name: Chengdu
desc: In 2008, a magnitude 7.9 earthquake struck 80 kilometres from Chengdu, killing nearly 70,000 people. Among the most devastating losses were thousands of children who died when their school buildings collapsed — while nearby government offices remained standing. Parents who protested were detained. An investigation revealed that contractors had used substandard concrete and omitted required steel reinforcing bars in a construction practice locals called tofu buildings. The government eventually paid compensation to some families but suppressed reporting on the scale of the construction failures.

name: Nanjing
desc: In December 1937, Japanese forces captured Nanjing and embarked on six weeks of systematic murder, rape, and looting that killed an estimated 200,000 to 300,000 civilians. The atrocity was documented by a group of Westerners who established a Safety Zone and sheltered 200,000 Chinese civilians — including a Nazi Party member named John Rabe, whose party membership may have deterred Japanese attacks on his compound. Rabe is remembered as the Good Nazi of Nanjing. Japan's official acknowledgment of the massacre remains disputed and is a source of ongoing diplomatic tension.

name: Wuhan
desc: In late 2019, a Wuhan ophthalmologist named Li Wenliang sent a message to fellow doctors warning them about a SARS-like illness appearing in patients. Police summoned him and forced him to sign a statement admitting he had made false comments. He was back at work within days. Li then contracted the virus from an asymptomatic patient, announced his diagnosis publicly, and died in February 2020, becoming a symbol of the suppression of early pandemic warnings. His death triggered an extraordinary outpouring of public grief and anger across China.

name: Ho Chi Minh City
desc: In April 1975, as North Vietnamese tanks approached Saigon, the US Embassy began a chaotic helicopter evacuation from its rooftop. The CIA station chief had compiled a list of Vietnamese employees and informants to be evacuated — but in the chaos, thousands were left behind. An American radio broadcast playing White Christmas was the pre-arranged signal for Americans to report to evacuation points. Pilots flew sorties until dawn, evacuating over 7,000 people in 19 hours. Thousands of Vietnamese who had worked for Americans were left to face re-education camps.

name: Luanda
desc: From 2002 to 2015, Angola's oil boom made Luanda briefly the most expensive city in the world for expatriates — a Big Mac cost $15, a one-bedroom apartment rented for $15,000 a month, and a hotel room could cost $400 a night. This in a country where half the population lived on under $2 a day. The wealth was concentrated almost entirely in the political elite connected to the ruling MPLA party, making Angola one of the starkest examples of the resource curse — the paradox where vast natural wealth produces greater inequality.

name: Ahmedabad
desc: In February 2002, a train carrying Hindu pilgrims returning from Ayodhya was set on fire near Ahmedabad, killing 59 people. Within hours, coordinated anti-Muslim violence broke out across Gujarat state. In Ahmedabad, mobs attacked Muslim neighbourhoods with voter registration lists used to identify Muslim homes. Over three days, approximately 1,000 people were killed and 150,000 displaced. A Supreme Court investigation later found that police had been ordered to stand down. The then Chief Minister of Gujarat, Narendra Modi, faced years of controversy over his government's response to the riots.

name: Kuala Lumpur
desc: In 1969, racial riots between Malay and Chinese communities erupted in Kuala Lumpur following a general election, killing between 200 and 2,000 people depending on which source you consult — the Malaysian government has never released official figures. The riots led to the suspension of parliament and the introduction of the New Economic Policy, which gave Malays preferential access to education, business licences, and government contracts. That policy, still in effect in modified form today, has shaped Malaysian society for over half a century.

name: Xi'an
desc: In 1974, a farmer named Yang Zhifa was digging a well outside Xi'an when his shovel struck a terracotta head. He and his brothers continued digging, thinking little of it. Local officials eventually called archaeologists, who discovered they were standing above the burial complex of China's first emperor, Qin Shi Huang — including an army of 8,000 life-sized terracotta soldiers. Yang Zhifa spent decades as an official greeter at the site, signing books. The dig is still ongoing; the emperor's burial mound itself has never been opened.

name: Hong Kong
desc: In 1967, Hong Kong was swept up in Maoist fervour inspired by China's Cultural Revolution. Left-wing unions called strikes and protesters clashed with British colonial police. Then it escalated: militants began planting bombs across the city — over 8,000 suspected devices, of which 1,167 were real. Letter bombs were sent to government officials. A radio presenter who mocked the protesters was killed with his young daughter by a booby-trapped gift package. The violence backfired, turning Hong Kong's population against the leftists and reinforcing their loyalty to the colonial government.

name: Hangzhou
desc: In 1121 AD, a rebel named Fang La used Hangzhou as the base for a peasant uprising that briefly captured six cities and killed an estimated two million people before being suppressed. The uprising was so catastrophic that it weakened the Song Dynasty just enough that it could not resist the Jurchen invasion that followed — leading to the fall of northern China and the dynasty's retreat to Hangzhou, which then became the capital of a diminished empire. A peasant revolt in one city effectively redrew the map of medieval China.

name: Shenyang
desc: In 1931, Japanese officers of the Kwantung Army stationed in Manchuria staged an explosion on a Japanese-owned railway line near Shenyang and blamed it on Chinese saboteurs — a false flag operation known as the Mukden Incident. Japan used it as a pretext to invade and occupy all of Manchuria within months. The Japanese government in Tokyo was horrified and tried to restrain the army, but the officers had acted unilaterally. The incident demonstrated that Japan's military had effectively become an independent political actor, setting the course toward World War II.

name: Riyadh
desc: In November 1979, 500 armed militants seized the Grand Mosque in Mecca — Islam's holiest site — and declared that the Mahdi, or messiah, had arrived. The Saudi government was paralyzed; Islamic law forbids violence in the mosque. They eventually requested help from French special forces, who were converted to Islam on the spot by a Saudi cleric specifically so they could legally enter the holy city. The siege lasted two weeks and killed over 200 people. The Saudi government executed 63 survivors and imposed sweeping social conservatism across the kingdom in response.

name: Baghdad
desc: In 2003, when American troops toppled Saddam Hussein's statue in Firdos Square in Baghdad, the event was broadcast globally as a spontaneous celebration. Footage later revealed the square was largely empty — a marine colonel had orchestrated the statue's toppling, and most of the jubilant crowd were followers of the then-obscure politician Ahmed Chalabi, who had helped convince the US to invade. The carefully framed camera shot became one of the most analysed examples of media management in modern warfare.

name: Santiago
desc: On September 11, 1973, Chilean Air Force jets bombed the presidential palace in Santiago as General Pinochet's coup unfolded. President Salvador Allende died inside — officially by suicide, though the circumstances were disputed for decades. A UN forensic team confirmed the suicide finding in 2011. The coup ushered in a 17-year dictatorship during which an estimated 3,000 people were killed or disappeared and 40,000 were tortured. Among the CIA documents later declassified, one described Pinochet's regime as a good beginning.

name: Surat
desc: In 1994, a plague outbreak in Surat caused one of the most dramatic mass evacuations in modern Indian history. Within 48 hours of the diagnosis, half a million people fled the city by bus, train, and foot, spreading panic across India. International flights were suspended and Indian goods were briefly banned in some countries. It later emerged the outbreak was relatively limited — around 56 people died — but the speed of the panic and flight revealed how quickly a disease scare could paralyse a modern city and cause economic damage far exceeding the medical reality.

name: Madrid
desc: On March 11, 2004 — three days before Spain's general election — ten bombs exploded on four commuter trains in Madrid, killing 191 people and wounding 1,800. The ruling government immediately blamed ETA, the Basque separatist group, and suppressed evidence pointing to Al-Qaeda. Millions of Spaniards, suspecting the government was lying to protect its electoral position, spontaneously gathered in streets across Spain holding signs reading Who did it? The ruling party lost the election three days later. It was the first time in history that a terrorist attack had directly changed the outcome of a democratic election.

name: Suzhou
desc: In 1860, the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom — a millenarian Christian movement that had already killed millions in southern China — marched on Suzhou and took the city. The Taiping leader believed he was the younger brother of Jesus Christ. To retake Suzhou, the Qing government hired Frederick Townsend Ward, an American adventurer, to lead a mercenary force called the Ever Victorious Army. Ward was killed in battle and replaced by British officer Charles Gordon — later of Khartoum fame — who recaptured Suzhou in 1863. It was a war almost entirely forgotten in Western history.

name: Pune
desc: In 1897, two brothers named Damodar and Balkrishna Chapekar shot and killed British plague commissioner W.C. Rand on a Pune street as his carriage returned from a garden party. Rand had ordered aggressive house searches and forced removals of plague victims across Pune, and was deeply hated. The brothers were hanged and became the first political assassins to be celebrated as martyrs in Indian nationalist history. The assassination haunted British colonial administrators for decades and inspired a generation of revolutionary nationalists who concluded that violence might succeed where petitions had failed.

name: Harbin
desc: From 1936 to 1945, a secret Japanese military unit called Unit 731 operated from a facility outside Harbin, conducting biological warfare experiments on live prisoners — mostly Chinese, Korean, and Russian — including deliberate infection with plague, cholera, and typhoid, vivisection without anaesthesia, and frostbite experiments. An estimated 3,000 people were killed. After the war, the US government granted immunity to Unit 731's scientists in exchange for their research data — a deal kept secret for decades. Japan has never formally apologised for Unit 731's activities.

name: Houston
desc: In 1900, a hurricane struck Galveston Island, just south of Houston, killing between 6,000 and 12,000 people — the deadliest natural disaster in American history. The storm wiped out a city that had been considered more promising than Houston as the regional commercial centre. In the aftermath, Houston quietly absorbed Galveston's trade, railway connections, and commercial networks. The tragedy that destroyed one city was the making of another. Within a decade, Houston had surpassed Galveston entirely and become Texas's dominant port city.

name: Dallas
desc: On November 22, 1963, President John F. Kennedy was assassinated in Dallas's Dealey Plaza. Lee Harvey Oswald was arrested hours later — then shot dead by nightclub owner Jack Ruby in a Dallas police station basement, on live television, two days after the assassination. Ruby claimed he wanted to spare Jackie Kennedy the ordeal of a trial. He was convicted of murder and died in prison. The sequence of events — a president killed, his accused killer shot on TV — generated conspiracy theories that have never fully dissipated in the 60 years since.

name: Nairobi
desc: In 1998, a truck bomb exploded outside the US Embassy in Nairobi, killing 213 people — 212 of them Kenyans, and 12 of them Americans. A simultaneous bombing hit the US Embassy in Dar es Salaam. The attacks were carried out by Al-Qaeda and were the first time most Americans had heard of Osama bin Laden, though his organisation had been active for years. The US responded with cruise missile strikes on training camps in Afghanistan and a pharmaceutical factory in Sudan. The Nairobi bombing was a dress rehearsal for September 11, 2001.

name: Zhengzhou
desc: In July 2021, Zhengzhou received a year's worth of rain in three days. Subway tunnels flooded trapping commuters who held their phones above the rising water filming themselves as it reached their chins. Twelve people drowned in the subway. A video emerged of passengers pressed shoulder to shoulder in a flooded carriage, water chest high, many of them making phone calls, remarkably calm. The image of the flooded subway became one of the defining photographs of climate change in Asia, shared hundreds of millions of times within hours.

name: Chicago
desc: In July 1995, a heat wave struck Chicago that killed 739 people in five days — more than any weather event in the city's history. The victims were overwhelmingly elderly, poor, and living alone. Many were found dead in apartments where they had kept windows and doors shut for fear of crime. The city had failed to open cooling centres in time and phone lines for the elderly were overwhelmed. The catastrophe led sociologist Eric Klinenberg to argue that the deaths were caused not by the heat alone, but by social isolation — and that poverty and fear were as lethal as temperature.

name: Los Angeles
desc: In April 1992, four police officers were acquitted of beating Black motorist Rodney King despite the existence of an 81-second videotape of the assault watched by millions on television. Within hours, Los Angeles erupted in six days of rioting that killed 63 people, injured 2,000, and caused $1 billion in damage. The National Guard was deployed and a curfew imposed. Korean-owned businesses were disproportionately targeted, exposing tensions between communities that the city had never formally addressed. The riots transformed debates about police brutality and race in America for a generation.

name: Johannesburg
desc: In the 1980s and early 1990s, Johannesburg's gold mines employed over 500,000 Black workers who were legally required to live in single-sex hostels, separated from their families for 11 months a year under apartheid's migrant labour laws. The hostels became recruiting grounds for political violence — Inkatha Freedom Party and ANC supporters fought pitched battles that killed thousands. The violence was later found to have been covertly encouraged by elements of the apartheid security forces in a strategy known as third force operations, designed to destabilise the transition to democracy.

name: Qingdao
desc: In 2008, just weeks before the Beijing Olympics sailing events were due to be held in Qingdao, the city's coastline was engulfed by the largest algal bloom ever recorded — 20 million tonnes of bright green algae blanketing the bay. 10,000 people were deployed to manually scoop algae off the water for weeks. Sailors trained in green soup. The algae was eventually cleared just in time — but the cause was the city's own agricultural and sewage runoff, an irony not lost on observers of China's rapid development and its environmental costs.

name: Melbourne
desc: In November 1975, Australia's Prime Minister Gough Whitlam was dismissed from office in a constitutional crisis unique in the democratic world — not voted out, not impeached, but sacked by the Governor-General, the Queen's representative. Whitlam had locked horns with a hostile Senate that refused to pass his budget. The Governor-General, Sir John Kerr, acted without warning and in secret. Whitlam appeared on the steps of Parliament House in Melbourne and delivered one of history's great political speeches: Well may we say God save the Queen, because nothing will save the Governor-General.

name: Alexandria
desc: In 391 AD, a Christian mob in Alexandria attacked and destroyed the Serapeum — one of the ancient world's greatest temples and possibly the last repository of the Great Library's scrolls. The destruction was led by the city's bishop, Theophilus, acting on orders from the Emperor. The event symbolised the end of pagan antiquity and the triumph of Christianity in the Roman world. Historians still debate whether the Library's destruction was a single event or a gradual dispersal over centuries — but Alexandria's murder became the defining image of knowledge lost to fanaticism.

name: Changsha
desc: In November 1938, with Japanese forces advancing on Changsha, Nationalist Chinese officials ordered the city burned to deny it to the enemy. The fire was started prematurely and in the wrong place, and the conflagration spread out of control. Over three days, 90 percent of the city was destroyed and an estimated 30,000 civilians died — killed not by the invading Japanese but by their own government's miscalculated scorched-earth policy. Officials responsible were tried and executed. The Japanese didn't actually capture Changsha until 1944.

name: Jinan
desc: In 1928, Japanese forces massacred Chinese soldiers and civilians in Jinan during what became known as the Jinan Incident — killing between 2,000 and 11,000 people after a confrontation between Japanese and Chinese troops. Japan occupied the city for two years. The incident is rarely discussed in international histories of the lead-up to World War II, but it was one of the first large-scale massacres of the conflict that would eventually consume Asia. China's inability to defend Jinan contributed to the sense of national humiliation that shaped its 20th-century politics.

name: Abidjan
desc: In 2006, the cargo ship Probo Koala illegally dumped 500 tonnes of toxic waste at 18 sites around Abidjan, including the city's main rubbish dump. The waste — a mixture of gasoline, caustic soda, and hydrogen sulphide — released a cloud of toxic gas across the city. 17 people died, 30,000 sought medical treatment, and over 100,000 reported symptoms. The ship was chartered by commodities trader Trafigura, which had been turned away from European ports and found cheaper disposal in Ivory Coast by exploiting legal gaps. The company eventually settled for $198 million.

name: Fukuoka
desc: Fukuoka is home to one of the world's most beloved culinary inventions: instant noodles. Momofuku Ando, inspired by long queues at black market ramen stalls after WWII, spent a year in a backyard shed experimenting before perfecting the recipe in 1958. He later invented Cup Noodles after watching Americans break his noodles to fit a styrofoam cup at a US trade show — he decided to make the cup part of the product. Today over 100 billion servings of instant noodles are eaten annually worldwide.

name: Singapore
desc: Singapore once solved its litter problem by making chewing gum effectively illegal. In 1992, after gum was found jamming the sensors of the new MRT train doors, the government banned its sale — the only country in the world to do so. Smuggling gum became a minor criminal offence. The ban caused international outrage, particularly from the Wrigley Company, which lobbied the US government. In 2004, a partial exception was made for therapeutic gum available from pharmacists — but only if you gave your name. Littering fines remain up to $1,000.

name: Kunming
desc: Kunming is home to the world's longest-running flower market — and the city supplies cut flowers to most of China and much of Asia. The altitude, mild year-round climate, and volcanic soil produce roses of extraordinary quality. By the 1990s, Kunming's Dounan flower market was trading over a million stems a day. Today it is the largest flower auction in Asia and the second largest in the world after the Dutch Aalsmeer market. Chinese Valentine's Day sends Kunming into a frenzy of rose-packing that can be seen from satellites.

name: Dar es Salaam
desc: Dar es Salaam is home to one of Africa's most unlikely financial innovations. In 2008, Vodacom Tanzania launched M-Pesa here — a mobile money transfer service that let people send money via basic text message without needing a bank account. Within years, millions of Tanzanians who had never held a bank account were paying bills, receiving wages, and saving money through their phones. The model spread across Africa and to India, transforming financial inclusion for hundreds of millions of people who banks had never bothered to reach.

name: Tehran
desc: Tehran has one of the most unexpected art scenes in the world. The Tehran Museum of Contemporary Art holds one of the finest collections of Western modern art outside Europe and America — Picasso, Warhol, Pollock, Rothko — acquired by the Shah's wife before the 1979 revolution. When the Islamic Republic took over, the collection was deemed decadent and locked in vaults. It sat there for decades, occasionally shown in rotating exhibitions, while its estimated value climbed past $3 billion. It is the greatest art collection most people have never heard of.

name: Barcelona
desc: Barcelona accidentally invented a new form of urban planning. In the 1850s, city engineer Ildefons Cerdà designed a grid of perfectly square blocks with chamfered corners — the distinctive octagonal intersections that make Barcelona's Eixample district visible from space. He mandated that each block's interior courtyard remain green and open. That second requirement was ignored almost immediately as buildings expanded inward. A century later, the city began reclaiming those courtyards as public gardens. Cerdà's utopian inner-block parks are still being slowly restored, 150 years after he drew them.

name: Ankara
desc: Ankara is home to the world's largest collection of Anatolian civilisation artefacts, including the oldest known wooden furniture ever found — a table and screens from a burial mound dating to around 700 BC. The Museum of Anatolian Civilisations holds objects from 10,000 years of human settlement in a beautifully restored Ottoman bedesten. It was named Europe's best museum in 1997, beating the Louvre and the British Museum. Almost nobody outside Turkey knows it exists, which may be precisely why visiting it feels like a genuine discovery.

name: Sydney
desc: The Sydney Opera House nearly didn't get built at all. Jørn Utzon's design won the competition in 1957, but engineers couldn't figure out how to build his signature shells for years. The breakthrough came when Utzon was peeling an orange and realised the shells could all be cut from the surface of a single sphere — making them buildable in identical sections. The project still ran 14 years late and 1,357 percent over budget, but when it opened in 1973, Queen Elizabeth II was greeted by a protester who threw himself into the harbour wearing a banner reading Australia Is A Fascist Country.

name: Hefei
desc: Hefei is home to one of the most remarkable scientific experiments in the world: an experimental nuclear fusion reactor nicknamed the Artificial Sun. In 2021, the device sustained a plasma temperature of 120 million degrees Celsius — eight times hotter than the sun's core — for 101 seconds, setting a world record. The goal is to eventually generate unlimited clean energy by replicating the process that powers stars. Remarkably, the facility gives public tours, and visitors can watch scientists in lab coats attempting to recreate stellar conditions inside a doughnut-shaped machine in a Hefei suburb.

name: Cape Town
desc: Cape Town is the only city in Africa where wild penguins live on an urban beach. African penguins colonised Boulders Beach in 1982 when two penguins wandered ashore and decided to stay. The colony grew to over 3,000 birds. Residents initially grumbled about the smell and noise, but the penguins have become one of Cape Town's most beloved attractions, waddling between sunbathers with the confidence of creatures who know they are protected. Scientists are now working to establish a second colony at a new site to protect the species from a single catastrophic event.

name: Casablanca
desc: Casablanca has a love affair with Art Deco so intense that urban planners now protect it as a heritage style. When the French built Casablanca as a modern colonial city in the 1920s and 30s, they hired architects who blended Art Deco with Moorish flourishes into a style called Mauresque. The result is a city centre of extraordinary decorative buildings — tilework, ornate ironwork, sweeping curves — most of them still in daily use as offices and apartments. Casablanca has more intact Art Deco architecture per square kilometre than Miami, the city most associated with the style.

name: Accra
desc: Accra has one of the world's most extraordinary funeral cultures. Ghanaian fantasy coffins — elaborately crafted in the shape of fish, aeroplanes, Coca-Cola bottles, mobile phones, lions, or whatever the deceased loved in life — have been crafted in the suburb of Teshie since the 1950s. A fisherman might be buried in a giant fish, a businessman in a Mercedes. The tradition was started by a carpenter named Kane Kwei and is now considered a major art form, with Ghanaian fantasy coffins displayed in museums from Paris to New York — though their primary purpose remains joyful, dignified burial.

name: Khartoum
desc: Khartoum is where the Blue Nile and the White Nile meet — two rivers that have travelled thousands of kilometres from Lake Victoria and the Ethiopian Highlands respectively. For a considerable distance after their confluence, the two rivers actually flow side by side without fully mixing, due to differences in temperature, speed, and sediment load, creating a visible line in the water between lighter blue and darker brown. Khartoum residents have watched this daily miracle for millennia from the city's waterfront, and it remains one of the more quietly spectacular natural phenomena visible from any urban area on earth.

name: Addis Ababa
desc: Addis Ababa is home to Lucy — the 3.2 million year old fossilised skeleton of an early human ancestor discovered in Ethiopia in 1974. Lucy, a female Australopithecus afarensis, is displayed at the National Museum of Ethiopia in Addis Ababa and is considered one of the most significant fossil discoveries in history. She was named after the Beatles song Lucy in the Sky with Diamonds, which was playing on repeat at the archaeologists' camp on the night of her discovery. The finding established that humans walked upright before they developed large brains — upending the prevailing theory.

name: Miami
desc: Miami is home to one of the most audacious art installations in the world: the Underwater Museum of Art, where sculptures by international artists have been placed on the ocean floor off the coast. The works double as artificial reefs, and over time corals and fish colonise them, making each piece a living artwork that changes with every dive. Separately, Miami's Wynwood Walls — an open-air museum of street art in a former warehouse district — is credited with triggering a neighbourhood transformation so dramatic that property values increased tenfold in under a decade.

name: Kabul
desc: Kabul is home to the Afghanistan National Museum, which has survived decades of war through one of the most remarkable acts of institutional loyalty in history. During the civil war of the 1990s, museum staff hid the most precious artefacts — including the Bactrian Gold, 20,000 ancient gold objects discovered in 1978 — in the vaults of the presidential palace and told almost no one. When the Taliban took Kabul and smashed much of what remained in the museum, the gold was safe. It was rediscovered by a locksmith in 2003 and is still largely intact.

name: Atlanta
desc: Atlanta is the birthplace of Coca-Cola — but the drink was initially sold as a brain and nerve tonic by pharmacist John Pemberton, who was trying to create a non-alcoholic version of his coca wine after Atlanta went dry in 1886. His bookkeeper Frank Robinson not only named it Coca-Cola but designed the famous cursive logo in his own handwriting, a logo still used unchanged today. Pemberton was so short of money he sold the rights for $238 before he died. The company is now worth over $250 billion. Robinson got nothing.

name: Philadelphia
desc: Philadelphia is home to one of the world's great and little-known art institutions: the Barnes Foundation, which houses the largest private collection of Renoir and Cézanne paintings on earth — 181 Renoirs and 69 Cézannes — alongside African sculpture and Pennsylvania Dutch ironwork, all hung according to a bizarre personal system invented by collector Albert Barnes, who also refused to allow reproductions, public catalogues, or art historians access. Barnes was so combative that the city of Philadelphia tried to take his collection away from him. It mostly failed.

name: Jeddah
desc: Jeddah is home to the world's tallest fountain. The King Fahd Fountain shoots water 312 metres into the air — higher than the Eiffel Tower — from the middle of the Red Sea. It uses seawater pumped directly from the sea, illuminated at night by 500 spotlights. The fountain was a gift from King Fahd to the city in 1985. At full force, it can be seen from virtually anywhere in Jeddah and from aircraft approaching the airport. On windy days, the spray soaks nearby roads and passing cars, which residents consider a refreshing feature rather than a problem.

name: Taipei
desc: Taipei is home to one of the world's great engineering peculiarities: Taipei 101, the skyscraper that was the world's tallest building from 2004 to 2010, contains a giant golden ball suspended from the 92nd floor to the 87th floor. This 660-tonne sphere is a tuned mass damper — it sways up to a metre in the opposite direction from the wind to counteract the building's movement and prevent swaying. During Typhoon Soudelor in 2015, tourists watched from an observation deck as the golden ball swung dramatically back and forth, doing precisely its job, visibly cancelling the storm.

name: Kyiv
desc: Kyiv is home to the world's deepest metro station: Arsenalna station descends 105.5 metres underground — so deep that the escalator ride takes five full minutes. It was built in the 1960s and is deeper than any metro station in London, Paris, or New York. The depth was partly for nuclear shelter purposes during the Cold War. Today the long escalator ride is a quirky tourist attraction, with buskers who play for the full five-minute descent. During Russia's 2022 invasion, the station briefly fulfilled its original purpose when thousands sheltered in it from missile strikes.

name: Dakar
desc: Dakar is home to the African Renaissance Monument — a 49-metre bronze statue on a hilltop overlooking the Atlantic, taller than the Statue of Liberty from base to torch. It depicts a muscular man, woman, and child striding forward from a volcanic rock. When it was unveiled in 2010, it caused a scandal: Islamic leaders objected to its muscular, partially nude figures, the cost was astronomical, and the Senegalese architect who designed it was North Korean. President Wade defended it enthusiastically, then caused a further scandal by claiming personal intellectual property rights to 35 percent of the revenue from visits.

name: Yangon
desc: Yangon's Shwedagon Pagoda is covered in an estimated 60 tonnes of gold leaf applied by devotees over centuries — along with 5,448 diamonds, 2,317 rubies, sapphires, and other gems set into the crown. At the very top sits a single 76-carat diamond. The pagoda has its own meteorological effect: on very still mornings, the golden mass heats the surrounding air, creating a column of warm air that can be seen rising from a distance. It is maintained by a committee that measures the gold covering annually and issues reports on any degradation — a gold audit for the gods.

name: Guadalajara
desc: Guadalajara claims to have invented both tequila and the torta ahogada — a pork sandwich drowned in chilli sauce so aggressively spiced that eating it is considered a test of local character. But the city's strangest claim to fame is its market: the Mercado San Juan de Dios is the largest covered market in Latin America, spanning 65,000 square metres across three floors and selling everything from wedding dresses to live turtles to bootleg electronics. It has its own food court, a leather district, a silver district, and piñata makers. Getting lost in it is considered part of the experience.

name: Phoenix
desc: Phoenix solved its summer heat problem with a peculiar form of community infrastructure: its network of canals, originally built by the ancient Hohokam and later rebuilt for irrigation, has been transformed into a 181-kilometre linear park system where residents cycle, run, and kayak through the desert heat. The canals keep Phoenix's agricultural fringe alive while simultaneously giving the city an accidental greenbelt. On summer evenings, the canal paths fill with people exercising at 10 PM to avoid the daytime temperatures, creating one of the world's most reliably nocturnal urban outdoor cultures.

name: San Francisco
desc: San Francisco's famous fog has a name: Karl. Named by a local Twitter account in 2010, Karl the Fog acquired 350,000 social media followers and became one of the city's most beloved unofficial mascots, posting updates on his movements and feelings. The naming reflected something genuine — San Francisco's fog is so consistent, so characterful, and so central to the city's identity that residents speak of it almost as a neighbour. The fog is generated when the cold Pacific current meets warm inland air, and it rolls through the Golden Gate with predictable daily drama.

name: Seattle
desc: Seattle's Pike Place Market has a tradition that has confused visitors since 1986: fishmongers throw whole salmon to each other across the counter, shouting orders and catching fish with theatrical precision as crowds gather to watch. The Pike Place Fish Company invented the throwing system as a practical solution to moving heavy fish quickly — then realised the spectacle was attracting customers. The method was eventually studied by a management consultant and turned into a bestselling business philosophy book about workplace enthusiasm. The fish still fly every day regardless.

name: Montreal
desc: Montreal invented the bagel — or at least a very good version of it. Jewish immigrants from Eastern Europe brought the recipe in the early 1900s, but Montreal bakers adapted it by using honey-sweetened water for boiling and wood-fired ovens for baking, producing a sweeter, chewier, smaller ring than the New York variety. The rivalry between Montreal and New York bagels is one of North America's most passionate food arguments. The two original Montreal bagel bakeries — Fairmount and St-Viateur — are still operating 24 hours a day, and Montrealers have been known to fly home with bagels in their carry-on luggage.

name: Monterrey
desc: Monterrey is surrounded by some of the most dramatic mountains in North America, and the city has built its identity around them. The Cerro de la Silla — Saddle Mountain — visible from almost everywhere in the city, has been climbed by an estimated 2 million people, making it one of the most-ascended urban mountains on earth. Monterrey's industrial elite famously combines a Texas-style work culture with an almost alpine outdoor enthusiasm: it is the only major Mexican city where hiking boots are considered acceptable footwear at business meetings, and where executives routinely schedule board retreats in mountain cabins.

name: Recife
desc: Recife hosts one of the world's most spectacular and least-known carnivals. While Rio's carnival is famous globally, Recife's Carnival of Frevo — named for the frantic, acrobatic dance unique to the city — has been recognised by UNESCO as an Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. Frevo dancers spin small colourful parasols as they perform physics-defying jumps and squats at extraordinary speed. The music is played by brass bands at a tempo so fast that musicians must practice endurance breathing. During carnival week, Recife hosts around 1.5 million people daily, with almost no alcohol-related incidents — because frevo is too exhausting to drink and dance simultaneously.

name: Fortaleza
desc: Fortaleza has built the world's most unexpected tourism product: kitesurfing capital of the world. The city sits in the trade wind belt, receiving consistent 20-30 knot winds almost every day from July to December. Professional kitersurfers from Brazil and around the world descend on the beaches of Cumbuco and Icaraí de Amontada, transforming small fishing villages into international sporting destinations. The sport has created a new economy for communities that previously survived entirely on lobster fishing — and the fishermen themselves have become some of the most skilled kitesurfing instructors in the world.

name: Belo Horizonte
desc: Belo Horizonte is the bar capital of the world by one measure: it has more bars per capita than any other city on the planet — one for every 300 residents. The city's bar culture is so institutionalised that the botequim — a casual neighbourhood bar serving food and cold beer — is officially recognised as an Intangible Cultural Heritage of Minas Gerais state. Local bar owners hold annual competitions for the best bar snack. The city also hosts the Festival Comida Di Buteco, a citywide bar-food competition that sends thousands of people on a crawl across 170 competing establishments over three weeks.

name: Medellín
desc: Medellín connected its hillside slums to the city centre in 2004 with the world's first urban cable car metro system — gondolas that glide over the rooftops of barrios that previously took 90 minutes to reach by foot. Journey time dropped to 10 minutes. The cable cars were so successful that the city followed them with outdoor escalators climbing the steepest hillsides, the world's first permanent urban escalator system. The neighbourhood around the escalators transformed completely within five years: new schools, libraries, and restaurants appeared, and property values quadrupled. Urban planners now travel from around the world to study the model.

name: Algiers
desc: Algiers contains one of the Mediterranean's most extraordinary historic districts: the Casbah, a UNESCO World Heritage Site of white-washed Ottoman-era houses stacked up a steep hillside, connected by a labyrinth of alleyways so narrow that neighbours can shake hands from their windows. The Casbah was built to be deliberately confusing to outsiders — its maze-like layout was designed as a defence system. During the French colonial period and the War of Independence, its layout made it almost impossible for French troops to navigate without local guides. Algiers' residents still know short-cuts through it that no map can capture.

name: Tunis
desc: Tunis is home to one of the world's most remarkable medinas. The Medina of Tunis, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, has been continuously inhabited and traded in for over 1,000 years — its souk layout, with different trades clustered together in specific streets, has barely changed since the medieval Islamic city was planned. The perfume souk smells of jasmine, the chechia souk (selling traditional red felt hats) is still making the same product it has sold for 600 years, and the stalls of the book souk contain illuminated manuscripts alongside paperback thrillers. Tunis's medina is one of the few genuinely living medieval cities left in the world.

name: Rome
desc: Rome's Trevi Fountain collects approximately €1.5 million in coins thrown by tourists every year — an amount that has been formally dedicated to a local supermarket chain that provides groceries to Rome's poor. The tradition of throwing a coin to guarantee a return to Rome comes from the 1954 film Three Coins in the Fountain. The coins are collected three times a week under police supervision. In 2002, a man was arrested for diving into the fountain at night with a wetsuit to collect the coins. He had apparently been doing it for years.

name: Milan
desc: Milan is home to the world's oldest shopping mall: the Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II, a magnificent iron-and-glass arcade built in 1877 connecting the Duomo to La Scala opera house. It contains one of the world's oldest McDonald's, a branch of Prada that has operated since the 1930s, and a floor mosaic of the Turin bull upon whose testicles visitors spin on their heel for good luck — a tradition so popular that the testicles have been ground into a crater and must be periodically resurfaced. The Galleria is the most profitable retail space per square metre in Italy.

name: Berlin
desc: Berlin has more bridges than Venice. The city contains around 1,700 bridges spanning its network of rivers, canals, and lakes — compared to Venice's 409. Most Berliners are surprised to learn this fact, because Berlin's watery infrastructure is less famous than its division, its music scene, or its street art. The city was deliberately built on a swampy plain intersected by the Spree and Havel rivers, and its expansion over four centuries required bridges at almost every street crossing. Today those bridges are maintained by a dedicated department of the Berlin Senate, which issues annual bridge health reports.

name: Lisbon
desc: Lisbon invented the world's first modern postal system. In 1520, Portuguese King Manuel I established the Correio-Mor, a national postal service operated by a single hereditary family, the Matos family, who passed the rights from father to son for two centuries. The system worked so well that European nations adopted similar models, making Lisbon's postal experiment the direct ancestor of every national post office on earth. The Matos family grew enormously wealthy from the monopoly and eventually had to be forcibly bought out by the Portuguese crown in 1797, which they did not take well.

name: Vienna
desc: Vienna's coffee house culture is so significant that UNESCO added it to its Intangible Cultural Heritage list. A Viennese coffee house is not just a place to drink coffee — it is a guaranteed right to sit undisturbed for as long as you wish with a single cup of coffee, a glass of water, and a newspaper. The waiter will not hurry you. Sigmund Freud, Gustav Klimt, Leon Trotsky, and Theodor Herzl were all regulars at specific Viennese coffee houses, using them as offices. The Café Central still serves coffee at the table where Trotsky played chess before the Russian Revolution.

name: Warsaw
desc: Warsaw has an extraordinary secret beneath its streets: a perfectly preserved medieval city that nobody knew existed. When archaeologists began excavating the Old Town in the 1950s to assist reconstruction, they discovered that the rubble of the Nazi-destroyed city had perfectly preserved the medieval street level below. Cellars, wells, and even the painted walls of 14th-century houses were found intact. The reconstruction of Warsaw's Old Town was so accurate that UNESCO awarded it World Heritage status as an example of historic reconstruction — the only such designation ever given to a rebuilt, rather than original, city.

name: Budapest
desc: Budapest sits on top of an extraordinary network of thermal springs, and the city has been bathing in them since Roman times. The Széchenyi Thermal Bath, opened in 1913, is one of the largest in Europe — an enormous neo-baroque palace where Hungarians play chess on floating boards in the outdoor pools while hot springs bubble beneath them. Budapest has 118 thermal springs within city limits and 15 operating public baths. The thermal water is piped into homes throughout parts of the city, meaning many Budapest residents have genuinely natural hot spring water coming out of their taps.

name: Prague
desc: Prague has one of the world's great mechanical clocks: the Orloj, an astronomical clock installed in the Old Town Hall in 1410, making it the oldest still-functioning astronomical clock in the world. On the hour, a procession of wooden apostles parades past two windows above the clock face, a skeleton rings a bell, and a golden rooster crows. According to legend, the city blinded the clock's creator after it was finished so he could never build a better one for a rival city. He supposedly groped his way back to the clock and damaged its mechanism in revenge. The clock kept running anyway.

name: Stockholm
desc: Stockholm built the world's longest art gallery: the Stockholm Metro. Since the 1950s, the city has commissioned artists to decorate each metro station individually, resulting in 90 stations transformed into permanent art installations — rock caves painted in vivid blues, stations that look like ancient ruins, ceilings covered in flowers. Over 150 artists have contributed over 70 years. The total length of the decorated network is 110 kilometres. Stockholm residents have become so accustomed to their beautiful commute that a movement exists to preserve the metro's art as strictly as any museum collection.

name: Oslo
desc: Oslo has the world's most comprehensive collection of Viking ships — three original vessels discovered in burial mounds near the city, including the Oseberg ship, built around 820 AD and so well preserved that its wooden carvings are still crisp after 1,200 years in clay. The ships were burial vessels for high-status Vikings, packed with everyday objects: beds, sleds, cooking pots, and even an elaborately carved cart. They were preserved because the Vikings were buried in blue clay that sealed out oxygen. The Viking Ship Museum displaying them is about to reopen in a new building specifically designed around the ships.

name: Copenhagen
desc: Copenhagen has more bicycles than people. The city has 390 kilometres of separated cycle lanes, and on any given day 62 percent of residents cycle to work or school — including the queen. The city's cycling infrastructure is so advanced that there are bicycle rush hours, bicycle traffic jams, and cycling superhighways connecting to the suburbs. Copenhagen has also developed a system of bicycle bridges specifically designed for cyclists, including the Cykelslangen — the Bicycle Snake — a swooping orange elevated cycle path over the harbour that has become an architectural attraction in its own right.

name: Amsterdam
desc: Amsterdam has more bicycles than residents — approximately 900,000 bikes for 820,000 people — and loses around 15,000 of them to the canals every year. The city employs a team of professional bike fishers who drag the canals with nets and cranes, pulling out approximately 2,500 drowned bicycles a month. The retrieved bikes — rusted, twisted, unidentifiable — are recycled or sold at auction. Occasional hauls include other canal-floor treasures: a 17th-century cannon, WWII ammunition, and countless scooters. The annual bike-fishing operation has become a tourist spectacle in its own right.

name: Brussels
desc: Brussels is home to the Manneken Pis — a tiny bronze statue of a urinating boy just 61 centimetres tall that has been the city's most famous symbol since 1619. The original was stolen multiple times, including by British soldiers in 1745 and French soldiers in 1747, both of whom eventually returned it. The city now has an official wardrobe of over 1,000 costumes in which the statue is regularly dressed for official occasions — including a Scottish kilt, an astronaut suit, a miniature version of Elvis's jumpsuit, and a full tuxedo for when dignitaries visit. The wardrobe is displayed in a dedicated museum.

name: Zurich
desc: Zurich has solved its waste management problem with characteristic Swiss thoroughness: the city charges residents for every bag of rubbish they throw away via a special official bin bag that costs around 20 cents per litre. This creates a powerful incentive to recycle everything else — and Zurich has some of the world's highest recycling rates. The system also means residents pay close attention to what they buy and how they dispose of it. The Swiss in general are so efficient at waste that they occasionally run short of rubbish for their waste-to-energy plants and import garbage from neighbouring countries to keep the furnaces running.

name: Athens
desc: Athens is rebuilding its ancient city from the inside out, having decided in the 1980s to pedestrianise the area around the Acropolis and create what they called the Archaeological Promenade. Today a 4-kilometre-long pedestrian walkway connects the ancient sites, largely car-free. In the process of laying the walkway, archaeologists found thousands of additional artefacts, including previously unknown ruins of ancient streets, temples, and workshops, simply because they had never been able to dig beneath the roads before. Athens is essentially being archaeologically excavated as it simultaneously attempts to be a functional modern city.

name: Bucharest
desc: Bucharest has an unexpected subterranean population. Beneath the city's streets live thousands of homeless people in the city's underground heating tunnels, a network of warm pipes built during communist times to heat apartment blocks. In the bitter Romanian winters, the tunnels offer survival to people who would otherwise freeze. Charity workers, priests, and photographers have documented the communities that formed in these tunnels — with their own social structures, hierarchies, and rules. Bucharest's underground residents have been the subject of documentaries that brought international attention to the city's extreme inequality and the hidden lives beneath its feet.

name: Helsinki
desc: Helsinki has the world's largest continuous network of underground tunnels dug into solid bedrock — over 400 kilometres of passages housing everything from swimming pools to data centres, shooting ranges, a church, and a park with its own lake. Finns have spent decades excavating beneath their city because bedrock is cheap to maintain and provides natural insulation. The Helsinki city planning office has produced an Underground Master Plan, a genuine map of subterranean Helsinki, which includes facilities for a full city to shelter during Arctic winters or — as originally planned — nuclear attack. Most residents use the tunnels daily without thinking about it.

name: Minsk
desc: Minsk is home to the world's most pristine Soviet time capsule. The city was rebuilt after WWII exactly according to Stalin's vision of socialist urban grandeur, and unlike most former Soviet cities, it has been maintained rather than torn down. The broad avenues, monumental Stalinist architecture, and immaculate public squares remain almost exactly as built in the 1950s. Film crews regularly use Minsk as a stand-in for Cold War Moscow. The city also has one of the world's few remaining Soviet-era department stores — GUM on Independence Avenue — still selling goods under its original fittings and counters.

name: Tashkent
desc: Tashkent has the most elaborately decorated metro in Central Asia — each station is a unique piece of architecture, themed to Uzbek culture, science, or nature, with intricate tile mosaics, carved marble columns, and painted ceilings. Built between the 1970s and 2000s, the metro was a Soviet prestige project designed to demonstrate what socialism could create. Photography was officially banned for decades — the stations were considered military infrastructure. The ban was only lifted in 2018, and visitors immediately began flooding Instagram with images of the extraordinary underground palaces that Tashkent had kept mostly secret for 40 years.

name: Almaty
desc: Almaty has one of the world's most remarkable outdoor rinks: the Medeu High-Mountain Ice Skating Rink, built at 1,691 metres above sea level in a valley in the Tian Shan foothills 15 kilometres from the city centre. At 10,500 square metres, it is the world's largest outdoor skating rink, and its altitude means the ice freezes naturally and stays harder than low-altitude rinks. Over 200 world speed skating records have been set at Medeu. On winter weekends, thousands of Almaty families take the bus up the mountain and skate under the snow-capped peaks before returning to the city for dinner.

name: Baku
desc: Baku has one of the world's most visually dramatic modern skylines: the Flame Towers, three glass skyscrapers shaped like flames that dominate the city's waterfront. By night, an enormous LED facade covering the buildings runs continuous animations of fire, the Azerbaijani flag, and seasonal imagery, visible from 20 kilometres away. The Flame Towers were designed partly as a reference to Azerbaijan's ancient fire-worshipping Zoroastrian heritage — the country still has eternal natural gas flames at the Ateshgah fire temple outside the city, where the ground itself has been burning for centuries.

name: Tbilisi
desc: Tbilisi's old town is full of natural hot sulphur baths — not built springs, but genuine geological vents that have been walled into domed brick bathhouses since the 5th century. The sulphurous water allegedly inspired the city's founding when a king's hunting falcon fell into a hot spring and emerged cooked. Alexandre Dumas visited in 1858 and declared Tbilisi's bathhouses superior to anything in France. The baths are still operating exactly as they have for centuries, with attendants who scrub, knead, and pour buckets of 37-degree sulphur water over customers who emerge pink and extraordinarily relaxed.

name: Beirut
desc: Beirut has an extraordinary tradition of reinvention. After 15 years of civil war ended in 1990, the city rebuilt its downtown from rubble into a gleaming limestone neighbourhood almost from scratch. Developers controversially bulldozed what remained of the old city centre, but also funded the most extensive urban archaeological dig in Middle Eastern history, discovering Phoenician, Hellenistic, Roman, Byzantine, Arab, and Ottoman layers all within a few city blocks. The finds are now displayed in an underground museum directly beneath the rebuilt Beirut Central District, so shoppers walk above 5,000 years of city history.

name: Amman
desc: Amman is one of the few cities in the world built entirely on hills — seven traditional hills, expanded to 19 today — which means the city has no flat streets at its heart, only valleys and ridges. This gives Amman a vertical character unlike any other Arab capital. The city also has an unexpected art scene: the Rainbow Street neighbourhood, carved into a hillside, has become a gallery district of repurposed Ottoman mansions. The Darat al Funun arts foundation is housed in a 1920s villa with a Roman temple in its garden — a casual archaeological discovery that nobody bothered to move when the house was built.

name: Damascus
desc: Damascus is home to the oldest continuously operating street in the world: the Via Recta, known locally as Straight Street, has been a main thoroughfare since ancient times and is mentioned by name in the Bible's Acts of the Apostles as the location where Saul of Tarsus recovered his sight after his conversion experience. The street is still a functioning commercial artery today, lined with jewellers, craftsmen, and coffee shops. Romans built their colonnaded boulevard on top of an even older road, which itself followed a path worn into the earth by thousands of years of foot traffic.

name: Tel Aviv
desc: Tel Aviv has the world's largest concentration of Bauhaus buildings — over 4,000 white-walled modernist structures built by Jewish architects who fled Nazi Germany in the 1930s and rebuilt the Bauhaus style they had been forced to abandon. The White City, as the neighbourhood is known, was awarded UNESCO World Heritage status in 2003. Many of the original architects arrived with nothing, worked in intense heat with local limestone, and adapted the European style to a Mediterranean climate — adding deep balconies, recessed windows, and open ground floors that the original Bauhaus masters had never imagined.

name: Muscat
desc: Muscat has a peculiar architectural rule: all buildings must be painted white or cream and must not exceed a certain height, preserving the city's low-profile, pale appearance against its dramatic volcanic mountains. The rule is enforced by municipal inspectors. Muscat also has no advertising billboards visible from major roads — another law unique among Gulf cities. The result is a city of extraordinary visual calm compared to its glittering neighbours. Sultan Qaboos also mandated that Muscat maintain green belts and plant millions of trees in the desert city, creating an improbably lush capital on the edge of the Arabian desert.

name: Dubai
desc: Dubai built the world's tallest building, the Burj Khalifa, but nobody knew how to pump concrete to its upper floors — no pump in the world had ever been tested to push concrete 600 metres upward. Engineers developed entirely new concrete formulations and new pumping techniques specifically for the project. They also had to pour the foundations at night because the desert heat would have caused the concrete to set too fast in daylight. The building sways up to 1.5 metres in high winds, its design having been specifically modelled on the desert spider Hymenopus coronatus, whose structure distributes wind pressure efficiently.

name: Doha
desc: Doha built the world's first air-conditioned outdoor public spaces for the 2022 FIFA World Cup — including open plazas and fan zones where giant cooling systems pumped chilled air into the open air through vents in the ground, keeping outdoor temperatures at a comfortable level despite the 40-degree Gulf heat. The technology, developed specifically for Qatar, essentially air-conditions the sky above a public space rather than a room. Critics called it environmentally absurd; engineers called it a proof of concept for future hot-climate cities. Either way, spectators watched football matches outside in December in what felt like a cool breeze.

name: Kuwait City
desc: Kuwait City is home to the Kuwait Towers — three giant spherical water towers built in 1979 that became the most recognisable landmark in the Gulf before Dubai's construction boom. The largest tower contains a revolving restaurant at 120 metres that completes one full rotation every 30 minutes. When they were built, the towers were celebrated as symbols of Kuwait's modernisation and oil wealth. They were damaged during the 1990 Iraqi invasion but repaired after liberation. Kuwaiti nationals consider them so iconic that the towers appear on everything from currency to school textbook covers.

name: Colombo
